summary.category: India's Six Major Physiographic Divisions and Their Characteristics
points: India comprises six major physiographic divisions: Himalayan Mountains (young fold mountains 2,400 km long with three parallel ranges—Great/Himadri, Lesser/Himachal, Outer/Shiwaliks—extending westeast with peaks averaging 6,000m), Northern Plains (vast alluvial plains of 7 lakh sq km formed by Indus-Ganga-Brahmaputra with four relief zones: Bhabar, Terai, Bhangar, Khadar), Peninsular Plateau (ancient Gondwana landmass with Central Highlands and Deccan Plateau bounded by Western Ghats and Eastern Ghats), Indian Desert (arid region <150mm rainfall west of Aravalis with sand dunes and sparse vegetation), Coastal Plains (narrow western coast and wide eastern coast with deltas and lakes), and Islands (Lakshadweep coral islands and Andaman-Nicobar chain).

Each physiographic region has distinct geological origins, topography, and climate: Himalayas are geologically young with steep slopes, deep valleys, and fast rivers; Northern Plains are recent alluvial deposits forming fertile agricultural land; Peninsular Plateau is ancient metamorphic rock with black soil; deserts are arid with minimal water; coastal regions enable maritime trade; creating a comprehensive geographical system.

Despite geographical diversity, these regions complement each other forming unified India: mountains provide water and forest wealth, plains serve as agricultural granaries, plateaus contain mineral resources for industrialization, coastal areas and islands support fishing and port activities, demonstrating how India's varied physical features create diverse economic activities while maintaining national integration through shared river systems, climate patterns, and historical-cultural connections.
